fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A precategory C is specified by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows: \boldsymbol { \lambda }_{ f } \colon 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad \boldsymbol { \rho }_{ f } \colon f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad \boldsymbol { \alpha }_{ f , g , h } \colon \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f = h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A precategory C is specified by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows: \boldsymbol { \lambda }_{ f } \colon 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad \boldsymbol { \rho }_{ f } \colon f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad \boldsymbol { \alpha }_{ f , g , h } \colon \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f = h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A functor F \colon C \to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a function \left \lvert F \right \rvert \colon \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert D \right \rvert giving the action of F on objects, where we shall write Fx for \left \lvert F \right \rvert x given x \in C;

for each x,y \in C a function F_{x,y} \colon C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \to D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Fy \right ) \mathclose {} giving the action of F on arrows, where we shall write Ff \colon Fx \to Fy for F_{x,y}f given f \colon x \to y;

such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions: F^{ 1_{ x } } \colon F 1_{ x } = 1_{ Fx } \quad F^{g \circ f} \colon F \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = Fg \circ Ff

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A functor F \colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F \colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f \colon C \cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \to C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \to C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible} \, F_0 \\ h_2 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \cong C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda x,y \mathpunct {.} { C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.

Let C and D be precategories, and let F,G \colon C \to D be two functors from C to D. A natural transformation \alpha \colon F \to G is given by a family of arrows \alpha _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } } D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Gx \right ) \mathclose {} equipped with a (necessarily unique) naturality datum \alpha _f \colon \alpha _y \circ Ff = Gf \circ \alpha _x for each f \colon x \to y.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \to C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \to C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible} \, F_0 \\ h_2 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \cong C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda x,y \mathpunct {.} { C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
A category is defined to be a univalent precategory.
A functor F \colon C \to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a function \left \lvert F \right \rvert \colon \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert D \right \rvert giving the action of F on objects, where we shall write Fx for \left \lvert F \right \rvert x given x \in C;

for each x,y \in C a function F_{x,y} \colon C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \to D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Fy \right ) \mathclose {} giving the action of F on arrows, where we shall write Ff \colon Fx \to Fy for F_{x,y}f given f \colon x \to y;

such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions: F^{ 1_{ x } } \colon F 1_{ x } = 1_{ Fx } \quad F^{g \circ f} \colon F \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = Fg \circ Ff

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: An arrow f \colon x \to y in a precategory C is called an isomorphism when either of there exists an inverse to f, i.e. an arrow g \colon y \to x that is simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
We shall write f \colon x \cong y to denote an isomorphism from x to y.
A functor F \colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f \colon C \cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.
A section-retraction pair in a precategory C consists of an arrow s \colon x \to y called the section and an arrow r \colon y \to x called the retraction such that r \circ s = 1_{ x }.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \to C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \to C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible} \, F_0 \\ h_2 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \cong C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda x,y \mathpunct {.} { C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

A precategory C is called univalent when for any x \in C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } y \cong x of isomorphisms onto x is contractible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Give an example of a displayed category B \vdash C over a precategory B such that the total precategory B.C is univalent but B is nonetheless not univalent.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B \vdash C be a precategory displayed over a precategory B. If B is univalent and B \vdash C is univalent, then the total precategory B.C is univalent.
A displayed precategory C over a precategory B, written B \vdash C, is specified by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each b \in B, a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert b of displayed objects over b that we shall write b.c \in C;
for each b_0.c_0, b_1.c_1 \in C and i \colon b_0 \to b_1, a set C _{ i } \mathopen {} \left ( b_0.c_0 , b_1.c_1 \right ) \mathclose {} of displayed arrows over i;
for each b.c \in C a displayed identity arrow 1_{ b } . 1_{ c } \colon b.c \to b.c;

for each i.f \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1 and j.g \colon b_1.c_1 \to b_2.c_2, a displayed composite arrow \mathopen {} \left ( j \circ i \right ) \mathclose {} \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1;
such that composition is associative and unital with respect to displayed identity arrows.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B \vdash C be a displayed precategory. We define the fiber of C over b \in B to be the precategory b.C whose objects are given by displayed objects b.c \in C and whose arrows b.c_0 \to b.c_1 are given by vertical displayed arrows 1_{ b } .f \colon b.c_0 \to b.c_1, with identities and compositions inherited from C.
